name: Cap of Invisibility
description: The Cap of Invisibility, also known as the Helmet of Hades or Helm of Darkness (κυνέη, kunée), is a powerful artifact from Greek mythology. Crafted by the Cyclopes, the cap grants the wearer complete invisibility. It was primarily associated with Hades, the god of the underworld, who used it to move unseen throughout the world, both mortal and divine. While Hades was its most famous owner, the cap was also used by other gods and heroes, often for strategic advantages in battles or covert missions. It is a symbol of stealth, deception, and the power to remain undetected. The ability to become invisible offered significant advantages, allowing the wearer to bypass enemies, eavesdrop on conversations, and influence events without being noticed. The cap’s power was absolute, rendering the wearer completely undetectable to both mortal eyes and divine perception, making it a coveted and feared item in the mythological landscape. Its use often resulted in significant shifts in power dynamics and outcomes of conflicts. The legend of the Cap of Invisibility highlights the Greek fascination with the unseen and the potential impact of hidden influence.
subtitle: Grants the wearer complete invisibility.
origin: Forged by the Cyclopes for Hades after their release from Tartarus.
currentLocation: Unknown, lost to time and myth.

keyMyths.title: Perseus and Medusa
keyMyths.description: Perseus used the Cap of Invisibility, along with other divine gifts, to sneak up on Medusa and behead her without being turned to stone. This allowed him to complete his quest for Polydectes.

keyMyths.title: Hades' Undetected Movements
keyMyths.description: Hades frequently used the Cap of Invisibility to travel between the Underworld and the mortal realm unseen, observing the living and ensuring the natural order was maintained. This allowed him to fulfill his duties without interference.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Mentions the Cyclopes forging powerful weapons for the gods.
sources.text: Mentions the Cyclopes forging powerful weapons for the gods.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: Describes Perseus's use of the Cap of Invisibility in his quest.
